rounds.reason: PyTorch and TensorFlow both executed successfully with identical results for the given 5x5 boolean condition and float64 x/y tensors. However, the current test case only covers a basic, well-behaved scenario (square 2D tensor, no broadcasting, no edge cases). To improve robustness and uncover potential framework-specific bugs (e.g., broadcasting mismatches, dtype promotion inconsistencies, or rank-0 scalar handling), we perform fuzzing: replace y with a scalar (exposing broadcast behavior), change condition to rank-0 (scalar bool) to test degenerate cases, use int32 dtype for x to verify cross-dtype compatibility, and introduce a non-square shape (3, 8) to stress dimension alignment — all while preserving semantic equivalence per official docs (both APIs support scalar/ndarray x/y and broadcast condition/x/y per their descriptions).

PyTorch and TensorFlow both execute successfully and produce consistent results for the given test case. According to the task requirements, when results are consistent, we must perform fuzzing (mutation) to increase coverage — exploring edge cases such as scalar inputs, rank-0 tensors, empty dimensions, extreme broadcasting patterns, and dtype variations (e.g., int64, bool for x/y where allowed). The original case uses 3D float32 tensors with nontrivial broadcasting; mutation introduces a rank-0 (scalar) 'x', an empty-dimension 'y' ([0, 3, 1]), and boolean 'condition' with shape [1, 0, 4] to stress broadcasting rules and degenerate tensor handling — both frameworks support scalar and empty-tensor broadcasting in torch.where/tf.where per their docs, making this a valid and high-value fuzz variant.
